title: Angular Basics
description: Core concepts of the Angular framework — components, templates, data binding, and directives.

sections.title: What is Angular?
sections.content:
Angular is a TypeScript-based web application framework developed and maintained by Google. It provides a comprehensive solution for building single-page applications (SPAs) with features like dependency injection, routing, forms handling, and HTTP communication built right in.

Angular follows a component-based architecture, meaning you build your UI by composing small, reusable components together — much like building with LEGO blocks.

Key characteristics

  • Opinionated framework — Angular prescribes how to structure your application, unlike libraries such as React.
  • TypeScript first — While you can use JavaScript, Angular is designed for and written in TypeScript.
  • Full-featured — Router, forms, HTTP client, animations, testing utilities — all included out of the box.

sections.title: Components
sections.content:
A component is the fundamental building block of an Angular application. Each component controls a section of the screen called a view.

A component consists of three things:

  • A TypeScript class that handles data and logic
  • An HTML template that defines what is rendered
  • Optional CSS styles that apply only to that component

You define a component using the @Component decorator:

@Component({
  selector: 'app-hero',
  template: '<h1>{{ name }}</h1>',
  styles: ['h1 { color: blue; }']
})
export class HeroComponent {
  name = 'Angular';
}

The selector determines the custom HTML tag you use to place this component: <app-hero></app-hero>.

sections.title: Data Binding
sections.content:
Data binding is the mechanism that connects your component's data to the template. Angular supports four forms of data binding:

1. Interpolation

Display component data in the template using double curly braces:

<p>Hello, {{ userName }}!</p>

2. Property Binding

Bind an HTML element's property to a component value:

<img [src]="imageUrl">
<button [disabled]="isLoading">Submit</button>

3. Event Binding

Listen to DOM events and call component methods:

<button (click)="onSubmit()">Submit</button>
<input (keyup)="onSearch($event)">

4. Two-Way Binding

Combine property and event binding with [(ngModel)]:

<input [(ngModel)]="searchTerm">
<p>Searching for: {{ searchTerm }}</p>
Two-way binding requires importing FormsModule in your component or module.

sections.title: Directives
sections.content:
Directives are classes that add additional behavior to elements in your Angular applications. There are three kinds:

Structural Directives

These change the DOM layout by adding and removing elements. They are prefixed with an asterisk (*).

<!-- Conditionally show content -->
<div *ngIf="isLoggedIn">Welcome back!</div>

<!-- Loop through a list -->
<li *ngFor="let item of items">{{ item.name }}</li>

<!-- Switch between views -->
<div [ngSwitch]="status">
  <p *ngSwitchCase="'active'">Active</p>
  <p *ngSwitchCase="'inactive'">Inactive</p>
  <p *ngSwitchDefault>Unknown</p>
</div>

Attribute Directives

These change the appearance or behavior of an element without altering the DOM structure:

<div [ngClass]="{'active': isActive, 'disabled': isDisabled}">
<div [ngStyle]="{'color': fontColor, 'font-size': fontSize + 'px'}">

Custom Directives

You can create your own directives using the @Directive decorator for reusable behavior.

questions.text: What decorator is used to define a component in Angular?
questions.explanation: The @Component decorator marks a TypeScript class as an Angular component and provides configuration metadata such as the selector, template, and styles.

questions.text: Angular uses two-way data binding by default for all inputs.
questions.explanation: Angular components use one-way data flow by default. Two-way binding via [(ngModel)] must be explicitly opted into by importing FormsModule and using the banana-in-a-box syntax.

questions.text: Which syntax is used for event binding in Angular templates?
questions.explanation: Event binding uses parentheses around the event name: (click)="onClick()". Square brackets [] are for property binding, and double curly braces {{}} are for interpolation.

options: Iterates over a collection and renders a template for each item
questions.explanation: *ngFor is a structural directive that loops over an array or iterable and stamps out a template instance for each item. Example: *ngFor="let item of items".

questions.text: Structural directives in Angular are prefixed with an asterisk (*).
questions.explanation: The asterisk * is syntactic sugar that Angular uses to wrap the element in an <ng-template>. Structural directives like *ngIf, *ngFor, and *ngSwitch all use this prefix.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a built-in Angular directive?
questions.explanation: ngClick does not exist in Angular. Click events are handled using event binding: (click)="handler()". Note that ng-click was used in AngularJS (version 1.x), which is a completely different framework.

questions.text: Angular is written in JavaScript and only supports JavaScript applications.
questions.explanation: Angular is written in TypeScript and is designed as a TypeScript-first framework. While it's technically possible to use plain JavaScript, the entire ecosystem — CLI, documentation, and tooling — is built around TypeScript.

questions.text: What does the 'selector' property in @Component define?
options: The URL route for the component
The custom HTML tag used to embed the component
The CSS class applied to the component
The name of the TypeScript class
questions.explanation: The selector defines the custom HTML tag name (like 'app-hero') that you use in templates to render the component: <app-hero></app-hero>.
